county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Boise is the state capital. Home to Idaho's largest city and the Basque community.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ada County Recorder in Boise maintains deed and land records from 1864, making it one of the oldest continuous record sets in Idaho. The Idaho State Historical Society and State Archives in Boise hold territorial records, governor's papers, and state vital records indexes. Boise has the largest Basque community in the US outside the Basque Country — the Basque Museum and Cultural Center on Grove Street has immigration records, boarding house registers, and family files documenting sheepherders who arrived from the 1890s through the 1960s. The Idaho State Library has Idaho city directories (Boise from 1891) and territorial census fragments. Boise Barracks military post records (1863-1912) are at NARA. Ada County also has strong Chinese community records from the 1860s-1880s mining era.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1864 to present. County Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1864. County Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1947.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from Washington County. Remote mountain county in west-central Idaho.
county_metadata.research_tips: Adams County Recorder in Council holds deed records from 1911. Council is a small town in the Weiser River valley, and Adams County has always been sparsely populated. The Adams County Historical Society preserves pioneer family photographs and records. Timber and ranching dominated the local economy — US Forest Service records for the Payette and Boise National Forests at NARA Seattle document homesteaders and timber claims in the region. Check Washington County for pre-1911 records. Mining claims in the Seven Devils and adjacent areas generated BLM records. The Meadows Valley attracted early settlers; school district records and church registers provide family documentation. The Council newspaper archives are a key local genealogy source.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Bingham County. Contains Pocatello, Idaho's second railroad hub. Adjacent to Fort Hall Indian Reservation (Shoshone-Bannock).
county_metadata.research_tips: Bannock County Recorder in Pocatello has deed records from 1893. Pocatello was a major railroad junction for the Oregon Short Line and Union Pacific, and railroad employment records at the Union Pacific Historical Collection identify workers. Fort Hall Indian Reservation (Shoshone-Bannock Tribes) borders the county — BIA Fort Hall Agency records at NARA Denver include census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, and school files. Idaho State University in Pocatello holds regional manuscript collections. LDS ward records are important for some Bannock County families. Check Bingham County for pre-1893 records. The Bannock County Historical Society museum preserves railroad-era and pioneer family records. Pocatello newspapers (Idaho State Journal) from the 1890s are on microfilm at the Idaho State Historical Society.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 from Oneida County. Southeastern Idaho county settled by Mormon pioneers. Bear Lake straddles the Idaho-Utah border.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bear Lake County Recorder in Paris holds deed records from 1875. Bear Lake County is one of Idaho's earliest settled areas — Mormon colonists from Utah arrived in 1863 under Brigham Young's direction, making the Bear Lake Valley the first permanent Euro-American settlement corridor in Idaho. LDS ward and stake records are the primary genealogical source for this county, and many are indexed on FamilySearch. Paris Stake was organized in 1877; records of the Paris, St. Charles, Montpelier, Georgetown, and other wards document virtually every family. The Bear Lake County Courthouse was the first in the territory. Montpelier was an Oregon Short Line railroad town — railroad records supplement church sources. The Bear Lake Pioneer Museum in Paris has family files and pioneer histories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1915 from Kootenai County. Northern Idaho timber county. Contains part of the Coeur d'Alene Indian Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Benewah County Recorder in St. Maries has deed records from 1915. The timber industry dominated Benewah County — the St. Joe River was a major log drive route, and lumber company records at the University of Idaho Library Special Collections identify workers and their families. Part of the Coeur d'Alene Indian Reservation is in Benewah County; BIA records at NARA Seattle include census rolls and allotment records for the Coeur d'Alene Tribe. Sacred Heart Mission (DeSmet) records from the 1840s at Gonzaga University document early Coeur d'Alene tribal members. Check Kootenai County for pre-1915 records. The St. Maries Gazette newspaper archives document the logging community. White pine logging operations generated employment records that trace immigrant Finnish and Scandinavian workers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1885 from Oneida County. Large county in southeastern Idaho. Adjacent to Fort Hall Indian Reservation. Strong Mormon settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bingham County Recorder in Blackfoot holds deed records from 1885. Bingham County is one of Idaho's largest counties and was the parent county for several southeastern Idaho counties. Fort Hall Indian Reservation (Shoshone-Bannock Tribes) is partially within the county — BIA records at NARA Denver document tribal members. Blackfoot was an Oregon Short Line railroad town. LDS ward records are essential for many Bingham County families — multiple wards were organized in the 1880s-1890s. The Bingham County Historical Museum in Blackfoot has pioneer family files and photographs. Sugar beet farming attracted Japanese and Mexican laborers in the early 1900s; agricultural labor records may survive in county and company files. Idaho National Laboratory (originally the National Reactor Testing Station, 1949) in neighboring Butte County drew workers from Bingham County — AEC/DOE personnel records at NARA document employees.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1895 from Alturas County (extinct). Wood River Valley mining and ranching county. Home to Sun Valley resort.
county_metadata.research_tips: Blaine County Recorder in Hailey holds deed records from 1895. The Wood River Valley experienced a major silver and lead mining boom in the 1880s — Hailey, Ketchum, and Bellevue were mining boomtowns. Mining district records and mine company employment records at the Idaho State Historical Society (ISHS) document miners from Cornwall, Ireland, Scandinavia, and China. Alturas County (the parent county, extinct 1895) records transferred to Blaine County. Sun Valley Lodge opened in 1936, creating a resort economy. Basque sheepherders worked the surrounding ranges; Basque boarding house records and sheep company employment files document this community. Ernest Hemingway's connections brought literary figures to the area. The Blaine County Historical Museum in Hailey has mining-era family files. Check ISHS for Alturas County territorial records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Idaho City was the largest city in the Northwest during the 1860s gold rush. Not the same as the city of Boise (which is in Ada County).
county_metadata.research_tips: Boise County Recorder in Idaho City holds deed records from 1864 — among the oldest in Idaho. Idaho City was the center of the Boise Basin gold rush (1862-1870s) and was briefly the largest city in the Pacific Northwest. Mining district records at the Idaho State Historical Society document thousands of placer and lode claims. The Chinese community in Idaho City was substantial — Chinese miners' records, tax lists, and cemetery records provide documentation for this community. The Boise Basin Historical Museum in Idaho City has gold rush-era family files and photographs. Several devastating fires in Idaho City (1865, 1867, 1868, 1871) destroyed some early records, but county records were largely preserved. Territorial census fragments from the 1860s at ISHS enumerate gold rush residents. The Boise Basin was the proving ground for Idaho's earliest legal and governmental institutions.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1907 from Kootenai County. Northern Idaho county on Lake Pend Oreille. Timber and railroad center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bonner County Recorder in Sandpoint holds deed records from 1907. Sandpoint was a major Northern Pacific Railroad town on Lake Pend Oreille — railroad employment records and land sale records at the Minnesota Historical Society and ISHS identify early settlers. The Bonner County Historical Society museum has pioneer family collections and logging-era photographs. Timber was the dominant industry; Humbird Lumber Company, Dalkena Lumber Company, and Pack River Lumber Company records at the University of Idaho Library identify workers. Check Kootenai County for pre-1907 records. The Kalispel (Pend d'Oreille) people historically inhabited the area; BIA records at NARA Seattle document tribal members. Hope, Clark Fork, and Priest Lake communities have distinct settlement histories. The Sandpoint newspaper archives on microfilm at ISHS document the logging and railroad community.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from Bingham County. Eastern Idaho's largest city, Idaho Falls. Strong LDS population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bonneville County Recorder in Idaho Falls has deed records from 1911. Idaho Falls (originally Eagle Rock) grew as an Oregon Short Line railroad town and later as the service center for the Idaho National Laboratory. LDS ward and stake records are primary genealogical sources — Idaho Falls was home to the first LDS temple in Idaho (dedicated 1945), and multiple wards were organized from the 1890s. Many records are indexed on FamilySearch. Check Bingham County for pre-1911 records. The Bonneville County Historical Society has pioneer family files. The Museum of Idaho in Idaho Falls has regional history collections. Idaho National Laboratory (formerly National Reactor Testing Station, 1949) drew scientists and engineers from across the country — AEC/DOE personnel records at NARA document employees. The Idaho Falls Post Register newspaper (1880s+) is on microfilm at ISHS.
